intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Devika is an autonomous software-engineering agent that takes a natural-language project request, decomposes it into a plan, writes code into a per-project workspace, and may run that code to validate it. The remit permits planning, code generation, web research via configured search providers, and inference against operator-configured LLM providers; it scopes all file I/O to the current project's work directory and treats user prompts and retrieved web content as untrusted input that must be screened for instruction injection. Devika MUST NOT execute shell commands outside a sandboxed runner, MUST NOT deploy to production, MUST NOT install packages without explicit user confirmation, and MUST NOT contact external services beyond the LLM and search providers.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask + Socket.IO application orchestrating eleven specialized sub-agents (planner, researcher, coder, action, runner, feature, patcher, reporter, decision, formatter, internal_monologue) under src/agents/agent.py, with pluggable LLM providers (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, Mistral, Groq, Ollama, LM Studio), Playwright-driven browser automation in src/browser/, SQLite-backed project and agent-state stores, and TOML-based configuration in sample.config.toml. The runner agent at src/agents/runner/runner.py executes LLM-produced shell commands via raw subprocess.run on the host. A src/sandbox/ directory exists with files named code_runner.py and firejail.py — both are 1-line stubs. A Netlify deployment integration in src/services/netlify.py is wired into the agent's action flow, and the WebSocket handler at devika.py spawns background agent threads with no authentication.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is sandbox-shaped-but-not-substantive compounded with policy declared in prose, zero code-level enforcement. The architecture document and the remit both describe a sandboxed runner that confines LLM-produced command execution; the actual implementation in src/agents/runner/runner.py executes those commands through raw subprocess.run with no allowlist, no approval prompt, and no isolation, while src/sandbox/code_runner.py and src/sandbox/firejail.py are 1-line stub files. The remit also forbids production deployment and forbids action on instructions embedded in user or web content, yet src/services/netlify.py wires a deploy capability directly into the action loop and not a single agent prompt or code path performs injection screening on the user message or the formatted web-search results before they enter LLM context or drive a tool decision.

A reader opening the workspace should start in src/agents/runner/runner.py, src/sandbox/, src/agents/agent.py (the deploy branch in subsequent_execute), and the WebSocket handler in devika.py: those four files together establish that the agent has an unauthenticated WebSocket ingress, no instruction-injection screening on inbound content, raw shell execution of LLM-produced commands, and an outbound deploy path the remit forbids.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Devika may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner.

rules.section: Scope Boundaries — What Devika does NOT do
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT deploy code to production.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, especially from unpinned versions or unverified registries.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Control MUST return to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output was not anticipated in the plan.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Every action — plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call — MUST be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: Each run MUST enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap to prevent runaway loops.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Devika must always do
rules.rule_text: The project knowledge base and memory MUST be scoped to a single project, with no content mixed across projects.

findings.summary: LLM-produced shell commands execute on the host via raw subprocess.run with no sandbox, allowlist, or approval gate
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner.

evidence.snippet: Runner.run_code() calls subprocess.run(command_set, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE, cwd=project_path) on each command produced by the LLM at line 119, with no allowlist, no approval, no sandbox wrapper
self.llm.inference(prompt, project_name) returns LLM-authored command strings that flow into command_set = command.split(' ') and the subprocess execution at line 137
Runner prompt instructs the LLM to emit raw shell commands such as 'pip3 install -r requirements.txt' and 'python3 main.py' for execution

recommended_actions: Wire src/agents/runner/runner.py to invoke a real sandbox — either implement the empty src/sandbox/firejail.py module to wrap each command in firejail with a read-write bind only to project_path, or run the runner inside a Docker container with the workspace mounted
Add a per-command allowlist driven by config and reject any command (or interpreter argument like -c) that is not on it
Insert a synchronous human-approval gate before each subprocess.run — surface the proposed command to the UI and require explicit user confirmation

findings.summary: Sandbox modules code_runner.py and firejail.py are 1-line stubs — the architecture-claimed sandbox does not exist
findings.description: The architecture document explicitly states the Runner 'Executes the written code in a sandboxed environment', and the remit explicitly forbids shell execution outside the sandboxed runner — yet the only files named for the sandbox role are 1-line stubs, and no other code in the workspace implements isolation. This is the canonical planned-but-not-deployed sandbox pattern: operators and reviewers reading the architecture document or the remit will believe a sandbox exists.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner.

evidence.snippet: File exists at the path implied as the sandboxed runner — 1 line total, empty stub with no class or function definition
File exists with a name promising firejail-based isolation — 1 line total, empty stub
Runner section claims 'Executes the written code in a sandboxed environment' — contradicted by src/agents/runner/runner.py which uses raw subprocess.run

recommended_actions: Either implement src/sandbox/firejail.py as a real isolation wrapper (firejail-net-none / firejail-private with bind-mount of project_path only) and route every Runner subprocess through it, or delete src/sandbox/ entirely and correct the architecture doc and the remit to state explicitly that no sandbox exists
Until isolation lands, gate the Runner agent behind a kill-switch in src/config.py that defaults to disabled

findings.summary: Netlify deployment capability is wired into the agent's action loop in direct violation of the remit's no-production-deploy clause
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT deploy code to production.

evidence.snippet: Netlify.deploy() creates a Netlify site and pushes the project_path contents via netlify-py's deploy_site API — the deploy is committed before any user-confirmation step

subsequent_execute() routes action=='deploy' to Netlify().deploy(project_name) with no approval prompt; the agent's Action sub-agent (src/agents/action/prompt.jinja2 line 18) is told 'deploy - Deploy the project' is one of the six valid intents it may select
Action prompt enumerates 'deploy' as a first-class intent the LLM may infer from the user's message

recommended_actions: Remove src/services/netlify.py and the action == 'deploy' branch from src/agents/agent.py:219-229 entirely
Strike 'deploy - Deploy the project' from src/agents/action/prompt.jinja2 so the Action sub-agent cannot select that intent
If deployment is a desired future capability, surface it only behind an explicit, separate human-driven UI flow with audit logging and an out-of-band confirmation — not as an LLM-inferred action

findings.summary: No instruction-injection screening on user prompts or web-retrieved content before they enter LLM context or drive a tool decision
findings.description: The remit requires that the user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection. No code path performs any such screening: the user prompt is concatenated into Planner and Action prompts verbatim, search-result content from Bing / Google / DuckDuckGo flows through Formatter into the Coder prompt verbatim, and browser-extracted page text is fed into the LLM via interaction.py. Combined with the auto-executed subprocess pipeline in finding PRAX-2026-05-23-001, this is the canonical external-content-to-RCE compound chain.
findings.policy_rule_text: The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision.

evidence.snippet: Template substitutes the raw user prompt directly into the planner system prompt — 'The user asked: {{ prompt }}' — with no sanitization or instruction-injection screen

research = self.researcher.execute(...) followed by search_queries() which fetches raw web pages and pipes Formatter output into the Coder prompt — no provenance or injection check anywhere in the chain
browser_content[:4500] (raw page text) is substituted into the LLM prompt that decides the next browser action — direct prompt-injection surface
Grep across src/ for any function named 'sanitize', 'screen_injection', 'detect_injection', 'classify_prompt' or similar yields zero matches

recommended_actions: Insert an injection-screening pass between user input ingress (devika.py handle_message line 79) and the first LLM call: classify the message, reject or quarantine inputs that contain instruction-shaped content, and log the decision

Apply the same screen to Formatter output and browser_content before either reaches another LLM prompt — quote untrusted content with clear delimiters and a system-level instruction that delimited content is data, not commands
Block the Runner pipeline entirely when the originating message or any retrieved content failed the injection screen

findings.summary: Package installs run through the unsandboxed Runner with no user-confirmation gate and from unpinned versions
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, especially from unpinned versions or unverified registries.

evidence.snippet: Runner prompt explicitly instructs the LLM to emit pip-install commands as part of its 'commands' array (example: 'pip3 install -r requirements.txt') with no user-confirmation step in the surrounding flow

Runner.run_code() executes every command from the LLM's commands[] array through subprocess.run in sequence — there is no inspection of the command verb (pip / apt / npm / curl) and no per-command approval prompt
33 direct dependencies are listed with zero version pins — any subsequent 'pip install -r requirements.txt' command the Runner executes pulls floating HEAD versions from PyPI

recommended_actions: Add a command-verb classifier in src/agents/runner/runner.py that flags any command beginning with pip / pip3 / apt / apt-get / npm install / yarn add / pnpm add / curl | sh / wget | sh, suspends the run, and requires explicit user confirmation before proceeding
Pin every dependency in requirements.txt to an exact version with a hash and migrate to a lockfile-based install workflow (uv or pip-tools)
Maintain a small pre-approved package allowlist in config so well-known dependencies of the user-provided project can install without prompting, but anything outside it triggers the gate

findings.summary: Unauthenticated WebSocket bound to 0.0.0.0:1337 accepts user-message events that drive the full plan-research-code-execute pipeline — external-input-to-RCE in one hop
findings.description: This is a compound finding combining the WebSocket ingress, the absence of injection screening (PRAX-2026-05-23-004), the absence of a sandbox (PRAX-2026-05-23-002), and the raw subprocess execution path in Runner (PRAX-2026-05-23-001). Anyone with network access to the host's port 1337 can send a user-message event that ultimately reaches subprocess.run on the host, with no authentication, no rate limit, and no audit. This is the canonical Step-7 compound chain at the highest severity.

evidence.snippet: socketio.run(app, debug=False, port=1337, host='0.0.0.0') binds to all interfaces — the WebSocket is reachable from any host on the network, not just localhost

@socketio.on('user-message') handler accepts any inbound socket event with no authentication, no session check, no origin check; it directly spawns a Thread that calls agent.execute(message, project_name)
CORS is restricted to localhost:3000 for HTTP routes but Socket.IO upgrade flows do not inherit that — the WebSocket handshake has no equivalent guard

recommended_actions: Bind the SocketIO server to 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0 at devika.py:209, matching the localhost-only assumption baked into the remit ('a locally run application')
Add a per-connection token check in the user-message handler — a shared secret loaded from config that the front-end attaches to every event, rejected at the handler if missing or mismatched
Introduce a rate limit on user-message events (e.g. via Flask-Limiter on the connection-id) so even an authorized client cannot drive the agent into a runaway loop

findings.summary: No human-approval gate for high-impact actions — code writes, command execution, deploys, package installs all run autonomously
findings.policy_rule_text: Control MUST return to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output was not anticipated in the plan.

evidence.snippet: subsequent_execute() dispatches action results to runner / feature / patcher / netlify deploy with no intervening confirmation step; the Action sub-agent's choice (action variable) flows directly into execution
save_code_to_project() writes every file the LLM returned in a single loop with os.makedirs and open('w') — no diff preview, no user confirmation
Inner loop of run_code() executes commands in sequence and silently retries failed commands up to 2 times with LLM-patched code — no user approval at any step

recommended_actions: Insert a synchronous approval step before each branch in src/agents/agent.py:subsequent_execute — emit a 'pending_action' event to the UI and wait for an explicit 'approve' or 'reject' socket event before executing
For file writes in src/agents/coder/coder.py:save_code_to_project, compute and surface a diff against the existing file (or 'new file' marker) and block until the user confirms
For Runner commands, require approval per-command (not per-batch) so the user can refuse pip install while accepting python3 main.py

findings.summary: Every dependency in requirements.txt is unpinned — 33 packages, zero version constraints, no lockfile

evidence.snippet: Lines 1-33 list dependencies (flask, openai, anthropic, google-generativeai, mistralai, groq, playwright, gevent, ...) with no '==', '>=', '~=' or hash specifiers — every install pulls floating HEAD
No companion requirements.lock, uv.lock, or poetry.lock in the workspace root — a re-install on a different day silently picks up a different transitive closure

recommended_actions: Pin every line of requirements.txt to an exact version with a SHA256 hash; commit a lockfile (uv lock or pip-compile)
Add a CI job that fails on any PR adding an unpinned dependency
Audit the current installed-version set against a vulnerability database (uv pip audit / pip-audit / safety) and capture the baseline

findings.summary: No adversarial testing, no security tests, no tests/ directory anywhere in the workspace

evidence.snippet: No top-level tests/ directory; no conftest.py anywhere; pytest-playwright is in requirements.txt but no test files exercise it

Only SWE-bench / capability benchmarks documented — no adversarial-input fixture set, no prompt-injection regression tests, no postmortem describing a security issue that drove an architectural change

recommended_actions: Stand up an adversarial fixture set under tests/security/ covering: (1) prompt-injection in the user message, (2) prompt-injection in a fetched web page, (3) instruction injection in retrieved knowledge-base content, (4) command-injection in the LLM's commands[] output, (5) path-traversal in coder file-write payloads
Wire those fixtures into CI so regressions surface before merge

When a fixture reveals a real issue, capture it as a postmortem in docs/security/ with the architectural change it produced — that record is what lifts the RAISE 'Build an AI Red Team' score off the floor

findings.summary: No structured per-action audit log — the only logging is a free-form text file and prompts are not logged by default
findings.policy_rule_text: Every action — plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call — MUST be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done.

evidence.snippet: Logger wraps fastlogging.LogInit emitting plaintext lines to a single shared data/logs/devika_agent.log — no per-project file, no structured (JSON) format, no schema

@route_logger only logs Flask REST entry/exit, not the WebSocket user-message handler that drives the agent; agent decisions, subprocess.run invocations, file writes, and LLM calls have no dedicated log call sites
LOG_PROMPTS = 'false' by default — even the LLM prompt/response stream is silent unless explicitly enabled

recommended_actions: Add a structured per-project JSONL audit log written to data/logs/<project>/actions.jsonl with one record per action — fields: timestamp, action_type (plan|web_fetch|llm_call|file_write|subprocess|deploy), payload_hash, decision_outcome, approval_status

Instrument src/agents/runner/runner.py:run_code, src/agents/coder/coder.py:save_code_to_project, src/services/netlify.py:deploy, src/llm/llm.py:inference, and the WebSocket user-message handler at each call
Flip LOG_PROMPTS default to true (or replace with a structured per-call log that captures prompt+response hashes by default and full text behind a config flag)

findings.summary: No wall-clock or step-count cap on the agent execution loop — only a per-LLM-call inference timeout and a 2-retry bound on command failures
findings.policy_rule_text: Each run MUST enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap to prevent runaway loops.

evidence.snippet: Agent.execute() runs plan → research → search_queries → coder.execute → save with no wall-clock budget; the while-loop at line 328 (ask_user) sleeps 5s indefinitely until a user reply arrives
Inner 'while command_failed and retries < 2' caps individual command retries but the outer loop over commands[] is unbounded by the LLM's command list size
[TIMEOUT] section defines INFERENCE = 60 (per-LLM-call seconds) but there is no STEP_LIMIT, WALL_CLOCK_LIMIT, or TOTAL_TOKEN_LIMIT key

recommended_actions: Add STEP_LIMIT (e.g. 50) and WALL_CLOCK_LIMIT_SECONDS (e.g. 1800) keys to sample.config.toml's [TIMEOUT] section
Carry a per-run counter and start_time into src/agents/agent.py:execute and subsequent_execute; raise a RunCapExceeded exception and emit an 'info' agent event when either is hit

When the cap is hit, set agent_is_active=False and require explicit user input to continue (matches the remit's 'Human approval is required for ... Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run')

findings.summary: Operator API keys (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, Mistral, Groq, Bing, Google, Netlify) are loaded from a plaintext config.toml in the workspace with no vault integration

evidence.snippet: [API_KEYS] section holds placeholders for Bing, Google, Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, Mistral, Groq, Netlify — Config._load_config() copies this to a writable config.toml in the working directory on first run
_load_config() reads/writes config.toml in CWD; setters at lines 114-180 mutate the same file via POST /api/settings — keys live in plain text on disk, edited by an unauthenticated route
Project download zip is reachable at /api/download-project with no auth; any config.toml accidentally placed under data/projects/<project>/ ships in the archive

recommended_actions: Migrate src/config.py to read API keys from environment variables first and fall back to a 0600-permissioned ~/.devika/secrets.toml outside the workspace — never the project work directory
Add authentication on POST /api/settings and on the WebSocket user-message handler (companion of PRAX-2026-05-23-006)
Document an opt-in path for a real secrets backend (1Password CLI, OS keychain, HashiCorp Vault) and call out plaintext config as development-only

findings.summary: Coder file-write path is built from LLM output with no traversal protection — file['file'] can escape the project directory
findings.policy_rule_text: All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it.

evidence.snippet: file_path = os.path.join(self.project_dir, project_name, file['file']) — if file['file'] is '../../etc/passwd' or '/tmp/owned', os.path.join silently uses the absolute path, bypassing project scope
os.makedirs(file_path_dir, exist_ok=True) followed by open(file_path, 'w') with no normalization or containment check against self.project_dir

recommended_actions: Before the open() at src/agents/coder/coder.py:77, resolve os.path.realpath(file_path) and reject if it does not start with os.path.realpath(os.path.join(self.project_dir, project_name)) + os.sep
Apply the same containment check to Patcher.save_code_to_project and any other write call sites; the read-side check in src/project.py:get_project_files is a good template

findings.summary: Unauthenticated /api/get-browser-snapshot sends any file path supplied in the snapshot_path query parameter — arbitrary local file read

evidence.snippet: @app.route('/api/get-browser-snapshot') reads snapshot_path = request.args.get('snapshot_path') and immediately calls send_file(snapshot_path, as_attachment=True) — no normalization, no containment check, no auth
CORS restricts to localhost:3000 but the route itself accepts arbitrary local file paths; a user-message-injected link or a CSRF from a localhost browser session can pull any readable file on disk

recommended_actions: Restrict snapshot_path to a basename lookup inside config.get_screenshots_dir(): take the basename, join with the screenshots dir, realpath, check containment
Reject any request whose normalized path does not match the expected pattern (UUID-hex + .png as produced by Browser.screenshot)

findings.summary: Knowledge base table is shared across all projects with no project-scope column — memory poisoning in one project bleeds into another
findings.policy_rule_text: The project knowledge base and memory MUST be scoped to a single project, with no content mixed across projects.

evidence.snippet: Knowledge table has columns (id, tag, contents) — no project column; KnowledgeBase.get_knowledge(tag) returns the first row matching the tag globally
search_queries() creates a fresh KnowledgeBase() inside the per-message agent flow; the add/get methods do not pass a project_name discriminator

recommended_actions: Add a project: str column to the Knowledge SQLModel and a composite index on (project, tag); thread the project name through every add_knowledge / get_knowledge call
Backfill existing rows with a 'shared' marker and migrate them out individually rather than letting them keep matching new queries

rows.purpose: Captures REST route entry/exit (when LOG_REST_API=true), LLM debug responses (when LOG_PROMPTS=true), and ad-hoc agent.info() / .error() calls. Not action-level or structured.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. Devika sits at the Absent / Ad hoc boundary across the board: an explicit sandboxing capability is named in the architecture document and the remit but only ever exists as 1-line stub files; the agent's user-facing socket has no authentication, no instruction-injection screening, and no per-command approval gate; LLM-produced shell commands run on the host via raw subprocess; dependencies are unpinned; there are no tests; and what passes for logging is a free-form text file. The single 1 — Monitor Continuously — is awarded only because a log file is written at all.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The remit scopes Devika to per-project software engineering, but the runtime exposes a Netlify deploy path in src/services/netlify.py wired into the action loop, an unauthenticated WebSocket handler, and a Decision agent that the architecture doc says can git-clone arbitrary URLs — there is no code-level enforcement of project-scope boundaries, just LLM-prompt convention.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: User prompts and web-fetched content flow directly into LLM prompts in src/agents/agent.py and src/browser/interaction.py with zero injection screening, no provenance tracking, and no validation before the content informs an action decision.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Runner.run_code() executes LLM-produced commands through raw subprocess.run with no allowlist or approval gate, the sandbox modules at src/sandbox/code_runner.py and src/sandbox/firejail.py are 1-line stubs, the WebSocket handler in devika.py accepts user-message events with no authentication, and no input or output filtering is applied anywhere.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt lists 33 dependencies with zero version pins, the LLM Runner is allowed to invoke pip install commands inside the same subprocess execution path, and several declared providers (Netlify, multiple LLM SDKs) carry credentials whose handling is delegated to plaintext config.toml with no vault integration.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No tests/ directory, no adversarial-testing artifacts, no red-team postmortems, and the only mention of testing is the project's stated aim to score on SWE-bench (a capability benchmark, not a security one).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: src/logger.py writes a single free-form text log via fastlogging; the @route_logger decorator captures REST entry/exit but no per-action structured record exists for LLM tool calls, file writes, or subprocess executions, and LOG_PROMPTS defaults to false.
